paragraphs: The immediate result of this was a perpetual demand on him for information concerning the Other World, and for guidance during his life in this world. After death, these Chiefs must be faced, and the only things that they will consider are his works. The man who has led a life free from lies and deceit shall live after death like a god.

The text of one of the oldest chapters of the Book of the Dead (LXIV) is said in the Rubric to the chapter to have been "found" carved upon a block of alabaster from the south during the reign of Menkaurā, a king of the fourth dynasty, around 2500 BCE. As time went on and people sought to write long texts or inscriptions, they made great use of wood as a writing material, partly due to the labour and expense involved in cutting stone. The ancient Egyptians developed a method for creating a type of paper, now widely recognized as papyrus. The discovery of papyrus cannot be dated with certainty, but the hieroglyphic inscriptions of the early dynasties depict a roll of papyrus. The antiquity of its use is therefore likely to be very great. Among the oldest dated examples of inscribed papyrus, some accounts can be noted that were written during the reign of King Assa (of the fourth dynasty). These accounts were discovered at Sakkārah, located approximately 20 miles south of Cairo. Now that we have discussed the primary writing materials employed by the ancient Egyptians, we can move on to examine the different genres of Egyptian literature that have survived to this day. CHAPTER II THE PYRAMID TEXTS
The Pyramid Texts are the name now commonly given to the long hieroglyphic inscriptions cut upon the walls of the chambers and corridors of five pyramids at Saqqara.

The ancient knowledge was first memorized by the funeral priests, passed down orally from person to person, and then eventually written down once the Egyptians developed a writing system. This ensured that the information would not be lost as time went on. The Pyramid Texts provide us with much information concerning the religious beliefs of ancient Egyptians, as well as many isolated facts of history that are unique to this period. However, a significant portion of the passages remain enigmatic, as they describe states of civilization and conditions of life and climate that are difficult for modern people to comprehend. This religion promised all who followed it, regardless of social status or wealth, a life in the world beyond the grave after a resurrection made certain through the sufferings, death, and resurrection of Osiris, who was the incarnation of the great primeval god who created the heavens and the earth.

After the statue had been washed and dressed, a meal of sepulchral offerings was set before it. First of all, incense was burnt, and the priest said, "You are pure." Water was then sprinkled over the statue, and the priest repeated, "You are pure."

"During the performance of the next ceremony, in which incense of various kinds was offered, the priest said: 'You are pure.'" When the chief priest repeated the phrase "O my father" four times, the priest representing the king emerged from the bull's hide and sat upright. This act symbolized the king's resurrection as a spirit-body (sāhu). The priest, who was also the king, wore a special garment and took a staff or scepter in his hand. He said, "I love my father and his transformation."

Horus loves those who love Him. He then pressed the lips of the statue, and said, "I have come to greet you." You are my beloved son. The chief priest then spoke, saying, "I have freed my eye from his mouth, I have severed his leg." This meant that the king had escaped the jaws of death and that a severe injury had been inflicted upon the god of death. e Set While the ceremonies were taking place, the animals brought for sacrifice were killed. The adoration of Unas is in heaven. He has become mighty like Temu, the father who gave him birth. After Temu had given him birth, Unas became stronger than his father. This Pepi is one of the Great Offspring brought forth in Anu (Heliopolis), who have never been conquered by a king or ruled by chiefs. They are irresistible, and their words cannot be gainsaid. Therefore, this Pepi is also irresistible; he can neither be conquered by a king nor ruled by chiefs.

The young man suggested meeting the magician's wife in a specific booth or lodge in her garden. She agreed and ordered the steward to prepare the lodge for their meeting. He then recited certain magical words over the crocodile, saying to it, "When the young man comes to bathe in my lake, you shall seize him." "When the young man goes down to the lake to bathe according to his daily habit, Ubaaner said to the steward, 'Throw the wax crocodile into the water after him.'" The steward had taken the crocodile from its master and departed. It will be remembered that the late Sir Henry Morton Stanley was a renowned British explorer who led several expeditions in search of the source of the Nile River. He is best known for his 1871 expedition, which resulted in the discovery of Lake Victoria and the identification of the river's source as the confluence of two rivers: the White Nile and the Blue Nile. M Stanley was known as the "stone-splitter" due to his remarkable physical strength.

The wife of Ubaaner instructed the steward to prepare the small cottage in the garden for her visit. After leaving the lodge, he went and bathed in the lake. The steward followed him and threw the wax crocodile into the water. It immediately turned into a large crocodile, about 11 feet long, and seized the young man, swallowing him whole. While walking, Ubaaner asked the king if he would like to see a remarkable event that had occurred to a young peasant. The king agreed, and they proceeded to the location where the magician took him. Ubaaner told King Nebka the story of a young man who spent days in a lodge within the garden, conversing and drinking beer with his wife. The king replied to the wax crocodile, "Leave now, and take your due with you." The wax crocodile leaped out of the magician's hand and into the lake. It then transformed back into a large, living crocodile.

It swam away with a young man, but its fate remained unknown. The motions of the rowers as they rowed the boat would interest him, and the sight of the depths of the water, along with the beautiful fields and gardens around the lake, would bring him great pleasure. "The magician said, 'I will arrange the matter.'" The king inquired about the cessation of rowing, and one of the maidens explained the situation. Upon his promise to retrieve the ornament, the maiden uttered words suggesting she was confident in its recovery.

When Baiufrā had finished the story above, Prince Herutataf, the son of King Khufu, stood up before his father to speak. "Up to now," he said, "you have only heard tales about the wisdom of magicians who are dead and gone. It is impossible to know whether these stories are true or not." Prince Herutataf replied, "He is a certain peasant named Teta, who lives in Tet-Seneferu." With dignity and antiquated language, Herutataf conveyed his compliments before stating that he had arrived with a message from his father Khufu, who now summoned Teta to his presence.  "The sage replied, 'Welcome, Prince Herutataf; welcome, O one who loves his father.'"

When they arrived, Teta requested a boat where his children and books could be safely stored. The prince provided two boats, each fully crewed. Teta, however, chose to travel on the prince's boat, sailing alongside him.  "The king emerged from the grand hall of his palace, where Teta was escorted into his presence." He told her that these children should attain to the highest dignities in the whole country, and that the eldest among them should become the high priest of Heliopolis. The king's heart was heavy upon hearing those words. "Why are you so sorrowful, my lord?" Teta asked.

When Rāuser objected, saying his wife was ill inside the house, they replied, "Let us see her. We know how to help her." He said to them and Khnemu, who was with them, "Enter in," and they did so. They went to the room where Rut-tetet lay. After the birth of the three boys, the four goddesses and Khnemu went outside the house, and told Rāuser to rejoice, for his wife Rut-tetet had given him three sons. He had no other option but to ask them to bring barley from his granary, hoping they would take some as a gift for their own stores. They agreed, and the god Khnemu provided the barley.

When they arrived, Isis said to her companions: "How is it that we, who went to Rutetet by Rā's command, have worked no wonder for the children, whom we could have announced to their father, who allowed us to depart without begging a boon." They created divine crowns, similar to those worn by the Lord. e They hid them in the barley. They sent rain and storm from the heavens, and returned to the house of Rāuser. Carrying barley with them, they said to him, "Let the barley remain in a sealed room until we return." They placed the barley in a sealed room. The handmaiden told her that it was provided with everything except jars of barley drink, which had not been brought.

A few days after these events, Ruth had a quarrel with her handmaiden, and she slapped her. The handmaiden was very angry, and in the presence of the household, she said to Ruth: "Do you dare to treat me this way?" With the intention of telling the king about the three children, the handmaiden went to her maternal uncle, whom she found weaving flax on the walk, and told him what had happened.

The stories presented above are valuable because they contain elements of history. It is now well known that the immediate successors of the fourth dynasty, including Khufu, Khafre, and Menkaure, who built the three great pyramids at Giza, were the most important kings. These rulers delighted in claiming to be sons of Ra and spared no effort to make the form of worship of the Sun-god practiced at Anu or Heliopolis universal throughout Egypt. They have nothing to do with the worship of the gods by those who live on earth, and such prayers and hymns as are incorporated with them were supposed to be recited and sung by the dead for their own benefit. The author of the Book of the Dead's chapters was the god Thoth.

They do not all belong to the same period. Many of them refer to the dismemberment and burning of the dead, customs that were once common in very primitive times but were abandoned as soon as royal dynasties emerged in Egypt. One Egyptian tradition, dating back at least to the early part of the eighteenth dynasty, claims that Chapters XXXB and LXIV were discovered during the reign of Semti, a king of the first dynasty. Another tradition attributes their discovery to Menkaurā, also known as the Mycerinus of classical writers, who ruled the fourth dynasty. The Egyptians indeed had a Book of the Dead, utilized by kings and nobility, starting with the first dynasty. This text remained largely intact until the advent of Christianity in Egypt. It is unclear whether these chapters were part of the Pyramid Texts or if both they and the Pyramid Texts belonged to the Book of the Dead. However, it appears likely that the four chapters mentioned above [39] formed part of a work belonging to an earlier Book of the Dead than the Pyramid Texts.

During the middle of the 18th dynasty, pictures painted in bright colors, known as "vignettes," were added to the chapters. These illustrations are highly valuable, as they sometimes clarify or provide a clue to the meaning of obscure parts of the texts. These were ornamented with vignettes drawn in outline with black ink. Although the scribes who made them wrote certain sections in hieroglyphs, it is clear that they did not possess the skill of the great scribes who flourished between 1600 and 300 BCE. The last Recension of the Book of the Dead known to us in a complete form is the Saīte Recension, which came into existence around 1250 BCE and remained in use until the Roman Period. Let me come to you, as I have committed no wrongdoing; I have not borne false witness or done evil. Therefore, let nothing bad befall me. Be my deliverers and protectors; do not bring accusations against me in the presence of the Great God.

However, the subject matter of the works contained in it is thousands of years older. It is only their forms that are of a late date. This work is a liturgy, recited at specific times of the day and night in the great temple of Amen-Ra at Thebes, with the aim of warding off the monster Apep from blocking the sunrise. Apep was supposed to lie in wait for the sun daily, just before sunrise, with the intention of battling it and overthrowing it.

The words of this book are said to have been spoken by the god Nebertcher. e "Lord to the utmost limit," or God Himself Their rule was largely benevolent, primarily due to the fact that, in addition to their divine attributes, they possessed human-like natures and physical constitutions. All the gods gathered in the Great House. They formed a semicircle around it, facing Rā. Their heads nearly touched the ground as they bowed in reverence. Rā addressed Nu, the father of the first-born gods, and said, "Give heed to what men are doing, for those I have created are murmuring against me."

He said, "What would you do?" Let your eye (Hathor) turn against those who blaspheme you. Rā said, "They have fled to the mountains; their hearts are afraid because of what they have spoken." The gods replied, "Let your eye go forth and destroy those who blaspheme you, for no eye can resist yours when it goes forth in the form of Horus." Thereupon the Eye of Ra, or Hathor, pursued the blasphemers into the mountains and slew them all. Rā welcomed her upon her return, and the goddess replied, "The work of vanquishing men is dear to my heart."

Rā then said that he would be the master of men as their king, but not destroy them. At every festival of Hathor, "sleepy beer" was brewed and consumed by those who celebrated the feast. Thoth did so, and the spells which he wrote under the direction of Ra served as a protection for the servants of Ra, securing their aid from Keb, who became the sole lord of all living beings. Thoth was to be forever the representative of Rā in the Other World.

Meanwhile, Rā appeared in heaven each day upon his throne, but he had become old and was no longer able to speak clearly. His spittle fell on the ground as he sat. This figure she placed on the path that Rā walked on as he entered heaven after surveying the Two Lands (Egypt). Soon after, Rā rose up, accompanied by his gods, and entered heaven. As he walked along, a serpent struck him with its fangs.

She went to Rā and said, "What is this, O divine father?" The heavens weep with me. Then Isis said, "Father, tell me your name, for he who can utter his own name lives." I am Khepera in the morning, Ra at noon, and Temu in the evening. The poison of the serpent coursed through Rā's veins. His works offered no respite from its effects. Isis said to Ra, "Among all the things you have named to me, you have not named your own name." She searched him thoroughly, and her name came forth from his body and passed into hers. Rā hid himself from the gods. His throne in the Boat of Millions of Years remained vacant for a time. When the time came for the heart of the god to pass into Isis, the goddess said to Horus, her son, "The great god shall bind himself by an oath to give us his two eyes." e  When the great god had yielded up his name, Isis pronounced the following spell: "Flow poison, come forth from Ra."

Horus of Behutet and the Winged Disk

The legend of Horus of Behutet is inscribed on the walls of the temple of Edfu in Upper Egypt. Some incidents described in the text are depicted through large bas-reliefs. The events described took place while Rā was still reigning on earth. Horus returned to the Boat of Rā-Harmakhis, in the form of the winged disk that shone with many colors. He said, "Advance, O Rā, and behold your enemies who lie beneath you in this land." Rā set out on the journey, accompanied by the goddess Ashtoreth. He encountered his enemies, who were bound and lying on the ground. Rā gazed upon his slain adversaries and declared to the gods by his side, "Behold, let us set sail on the waters in our vessel, for our spirits are lifted as we have vanquished our foes on the land." The Boat of Rā moved northwards. The enemies on the riverbanks transformed into crocodiles and hippopotamuses, attempting to intimidate the god. As his boat approached, they widened their mouths, ready to devour both the boat and the gods within it.

After the slaughter, the bodies of 651 crocodiles were brought and laid out before the town of Edfu. When Thoth saw these, he said, "Let your hearts rejoice, O gods of heaven, Let your hearts rejoice, O ye gods who dwell on the earth." Many succeeded in escaping towards the north once more, but after pursuing them for a whole day, Horus overtook them and made a great slaughter among them.

The gods of Egypt, fearing the wrath of their defeated allies, sent a powerful force against the enemy. The foe, realizing their situation was dire, retreated to Lower Egypt, seeking solace in the marshy lands of the Nile's delta. Horus pursued them, and soon found himself among them. He spent four days wading through the water, fighting off those who attempted to flee as crocodiles and hippopotamuses. This slaughter occurred on the seventh day of the first month of the season, which was thereafter known as the "Day of the Festival of Sailing".

Although a man was slain, he reappeared as a great serpent, taking residence in a hole beneath the earth, unnoticed by Horus. Rā, however, saw him and gave orders that Horus, the son of Isis, take up the staff with the head of a hawk, stand at the entrance to the pit, so that the monster would never again appear among humans. For the moment, there were no more enemies of Rā to be found in the district on land. Although Horus spent six days and six nights searching for them, it appears that several followers of Set had taken the form of water reptiles and lay hidden beneath the surface, and Horus saw them there.

Horus maintained a strict guard over the tomb of Osiris in Aaru, for he had learned that the Smait demons sought to desecrate both the tomb and the body of the god. The blacksmiths of Horus, responsible for the middle regions of Egypt, encountered an enemy body and launched a fierce attack, killing many and capturing 106 of their number as prisoners. Shortly after these events, Rā discovered that many of his enemies were still at large. They had sailed in boats to the swamps surrounding the town of Tchal, also known as Zoan or Tanis.
Thoth recited the spells that produced fair weather, and spoke the words of power that prevented storms from rising. In time, the boat arrived at Nubia.
